one_line_takeaway: A VANET traffic-forecasting system that uses Inner Product Functional Encryption with k-anonymity dummy cells to let a TMC compute only aggregated per-cell driver counts (never individual locations), feeding a Conv-LSTM+Bi-LSTM+attention+squeeze-excitation model that hits ~10% MAE at a 60-minute forecast horizon on PeMS urban data, with sub-second decryption overhead for up to 200 drivers across 80 cells.
key_finding: On the PeMS D4/I980 Oakland urban traffic scenario, the final AT-Bi-Conv-SE LSTM model achieved MAE=7.94%, MAPE=8.5%, RMSE=11.03% at a 5-minute horizon and MAE=10.10%, MAPE=10.75%, RMSE=13.9% at a 60-minute horizon (Table IV, Stage 4), improving over Stage 1 Conv-LSTM (MAE 9.05%/RMSE 12.32% at 5 min; MAE 10.50%/RMSE 14.21% at 60 min) and notably over Stage 2 Bi-Conv LSTM, which was the worst-performing stage (MAE 12.57%/RMSE 18.8% at 5 min; MAE 14.31%/RMSE 21.16% at 60 min) before attention (Stage 3) and SE (Stage 4) recovered performance. In the cross-model comparison (Table V) against DCRNN, AT-Conv-LSTM, Bi-LSTM, SCG, AGFCRN, and PGCN, the proposed model achieved the best 5-minute MAE/RMSE (7.49%/11.03%, though SCG had a lower MAPE of 8.60% at 5 min and better MAE/RMSE at 5-15 min) and was the best performer overall at longer horizons, e.g. 60-minute MAE=10.10%/RMSE=13.9% versus AGFCRN's worst-case 22.31%/28.09% and PGCN's 18.06%/24.74%. On the privacy/overhead side (SUMO-simulated Greensboro, NC map, 8km x 8km area, 40 cells, results averaged over 30 runs): each driver's encryption key (Dk) totals 66 bytes; an encrypted cell report is 64 bytes per cell, so encrypting an 80-cell area costs 5.12 KB and about 50ms of encryption time; decryption for a scenario with up to 200 driver reports across 80 cells takes under 600ms.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a privacy-preserving location reporting and traffic-forecasting system for VANET-based Intelligent Transportation Systems. It combines (1) a novel k-anonymity scheme built on Inner Product Functional Encryption (IPFE) that lets drivers encrypt their presence in a geographic cell (plus k-1 dummy cells for k-anonymity) and lets the Traffic Management Center (TMC) obtain only the aggregated per-cell driver count via a functional decryption key, without ever learning individual driver locations, and (2) a hybrid deep learning forecasting model (an attention-based, squeeze-and-excitation-enhanced Conv-LSTM, 'AT-Bi-Conv-SE LSTM', fused with a Bi-LSTM branch for daily/weekly periodicity) that consumes only this aggregated encrypted-then-decrypted density data to predict future traffic congestion.
threat_model: Honest-but-curious model: the TMC (assumed operated by the Department of Motor Vehicles) correctly computes traffic conditions but may try to learn sensitive driver identity/movement patterns from received data. Individual drivers (or colluding groups) may try to infer other drivers' locations. External adversaries may attack the communication gateway between drivers and the TMC, individually or in collusion. The scheme assumes driver reports are already anonymized (anonymization mechanism itself is explicitly out of scope) and assumes non-collusion between the TMC and the Key Distribution Center (KDC), and that the TMC does not collude with drivers because it is government-supervised. Security claims (Propositions 1-3 in Section VI) are informal proof-sketches, not formal cryptographic reductions: they argue that (1) insider/outsider adversaries cannot obtain driver locations since ciphertexts require secret keys and TMC only recovers negligible-probability-distinguishable 0/1 values under k-anonymity, (2) ciphertexts for the same driver's different cells are unlinkable because each encryption uses a fresh random nonce plus k-1 dummy cells, and (3) drivers cannot decrypt each other's ciphertexts since each holds a unique encryption key pk_i.
limitations: The security analysis (Propositions 1-3) consists of informal narrative proofs rather than formal cryptographic reductions or game-based security proofs, and does not address collusion between the TMC and KDC beyond an assumption of non-collusion, nor formally bound the information leaked by revealing exact per-cell aggregate counts (which itself is a form of statistical disclosure for low-traffic cells). The anonymization of driver identity prior to encryption is explicitly stated as out of scope, meaning the paper's privacy guarantee is conditional on an unspecified upstream mechanism. The threat model trusts the TMC as honest-but-curious and government-supervised, which may not hold in all deployments. On the forecasting side, the model is only evaluated on a single POI on Street I980 (Oakland, District 4) and on freeway SR99-S sensors from District 10 (California PeMS14 data), so generalization to other geographies/topologies is untested within the paper; the model does not consistently dominate all baselines at all horizons (e.g., SCG outperforms it on MAE/RMSE at 5 and 15 minutes, though it degrades badly at longer horizons). No formal privacy-utility tradeoff analysis (e.g., how varying k in k-anonymity affects forecast accuracy) is presented despite k being a first-class overhead-controlling parameter.

datasets_used: SUMO-simulated synthetic dataset: real OpenStreetMap of Greensboro, North Carolina, 8km x 8km area divided into 40 1km x 1km cells, random trips generated with SUMO software, results averaged over 30 runs (used to evaluate encryption/decryption overhead)

PeMS (Caltrans Performance Measurement System) PeMS14 dataset: traffic data from 2001-2023 across California's major metropolitan areas from nearly 40,000 sensors at 5-minute (some 30-second) intervals; study used 183 sensors in District 10 on Freeway SR99-S and 12 sensors in District 4 on Street I980, Oakland (used to evaluate forecasting accuracy)
quantitative_results: Final model (AT-Bi-Conv-SE LSTM), PeMS Oakland I980 POI: 5-min MAE=7.94%, MAPE=8.5%, RMSE=11.03%; 60-min MAE=10.10%, MAPE=10.75%, RMSE=13.9% (Table IV)
Ablation Stage 2 (Bi-Conv LSTM, worst stage): 5-min MAE=12.57%/RMSE=18.8%; 60-min MAE=14.31%/RMSE=21.16%
Optimizer comparison (Table III): ADAM best with MAE=7.94, MAPE=8.50, RMSE=11.03; SGD worst with MAE=39.45, MAPE=62.73, RMSE=45.66
Cross-model comparison (Table V), 5-min horizon: proposed model MAE=7.49%/MAPE=8.5%/RMSE=11.03% vs. AGFCRN (worst) MAE=15.10%/MAPE=9.67%/RMSE=17.81%
Cross-model comparison (Table V), 60-min horizon: proposed model MAE=10.10%/MAPE=10.75%/RMSE=13.9% vs. AGFCRN (worst) MAE=22.31%/MAPE=15.53%/RMSE=28.09%, and PGCN MAE=18.06%/MAPE=13.89%/RMSE=24.74%
Encryption overhead: driver key size (Dk) = 66 bytes; per-cell ciphertext = 64 bytes; 80-cell area encryption = 5.12 KB and ~50ms
Decryption overhead: <600ms for up to 200 driver reports (D=200) across an 80-cell area
baselines_compared: AT-Conv-LSTM (prior attention-based Conv-LSTM, Zheng et al.)
SCG (STFSA Convolutional Neural Network Gated Recurrent Unit)
AGFCRN (Adaptive Spatial-Temporal Fusion Graph Convolutional Network)
